summary: Jeff Atwood examines the overwhelming dominance of Google in web search, using Stack Overflow's traffic data as evidence. Six months after launch, 83% of Stack Overflow's traffic came from search engines, with Google delivering 350 times more traffic than Yahoo, the second-place competitor. While acknowledging Google earned its position by 'nailing search,' Atwood expresses concern about the lack of competition and the implications of a single company controlling how people find information online. He draws parallels to the Microsoft antitrust concerns of the previous decade, questioning why Google escapes similar scrutiny despite its monopolistic position.
key_points: Google delivered 350x more traffic to Stack Overflow than Yahoo, making all other search engines effectively irrelevant
The search engine market has become even more lopsided over time - ratios that were 6-8x two years prior had grown to 350x
If every search engine except Google shut down tomorrow, most websites would see no meaningful change in traffic
The 'don't be evil' mantra and free products don't exempt Google from monopoly concerns that plagued Microsoft
Being removed from Google's index effectively means your website and business no longer exist
money_quotes: When we say 'web search' we mean one thing, and one thing only: Google.
Google delivers 350x the traffic to Stack Overflow that the next best so-called 'search engine' does. Three hundred and fifty times!
I find it profoundly disturbing that if every other search engine in the world shut down tomorrow, our website's traffic would be effectively unchanged. That's downright scary.
Where's the healthy competition? Where's the incentive for Google to improve? All I see is a large and growing monoculture that acts as the start page for the internet.
Calling the current state of search engine competition a horse race is an insult to horse races.
What we have here is a one horse race where all the other horses were shipped off to glue factories years ago.
Forget 'search conference,' you should be throwing a 'Google conference,' because there's no difference.
If Google, for whatever reason, decided to remove you from its search results, your website no longer exists.
key_insight: Google's search dominance has reached a point where the entire concept of 'search engine competition' is a polite fiction, and every online business exists at Google's discretion.
